heroOverview: Aphonia is the complete inability to produce voiced sound, leaving a person able to communicate only in a whisper or mouthed speech. It differs from hoarseness or dysphonia — in which the voice is altered but present — because the vocal folds either fail to approximate fully or are prevented from vibrating by inflammation, paralysis, or a functional (psychogenic) mechanism. Functional aphonia, in which the larynx is structurally normal, accounts for roughly 30–50% of cases seen in voice clinics, making it one of the more common presentations of conversion-type vocal disorder. Organic causes include acute laryngitis, vocal fold paralysis, laryngeal cancer, and severe gastro-oesophageal reflux. Recovery with appropriate treatment is good to excellent in most patients, particularly those with functional or inflammatory causes, though neurological aphonia can take longer and sometimes requires long-term management.

keyStats.prevalence: Voice disorders affect approximately 7% of the general population at any one time; aphonia (complete voice loss) is the most severe end of this spectrum
keyStats.demographics: Functional aphonia is significantly more common in women, with female-to-male ratios of 4:1 to 10:1 reported in voice clinic populations
keyStats.avgAge: Functional aphonia peaks in adults aged 20–50; inflammatory causes span all ages
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 17 million working-age adults in the US experience voice problems annually (NIDCD, 2024)

definition: Aphonia (ICD-10: R49.1) is the complete loss of phonation — the ability to produce voiced sound through vibration of the vocal folds. The term derives from the Greek for 'without voice'. It is distinguished from dysphonia (impaired but present voice) by the total absence of normal voicing, though a whisper or compensatory falsetto may be preserved. Aphonia falls under speech-language pathology and otolaryngology (ear, nose, and throat specialist care). Mechanistically it arises when the vocal folds cannot vibrate normally: they may be inflamed and swollen shut, paralysed and gapping, mechanically obstructed by a mass, or held open by a psychogenic or conversion process that prevents normal adduction despite intact neurological pathways. Functional aphonia — the last of these — is diagnosed when laryngoscopy reveals normal or near-normal vocal fold structure and movement during non-phonatory tasks such as coughing or laughing, but phonation cannot be initiated voluntarily. Organic aphonia, by contrast, reflects identifiable structural or neurological pathology. Accurate classification matters because the treatments differ substantially: voice therapy and psychological support for functional cases versus medical, surgical, or neurological management for organic causes.

typesClassification.description: The most common category in voice clinics: laryngoscopy is normal or shows only minor non-specific changes; the patient can cough, laugh, or cry with voice but cannot phonate on demand. It is classified as a functional neurological symptom disorder and responds well to focused voice therapy.

Caused by acute laryngitis, usually viral, which swells and inflames the vocal folds so severely that vibration is impossible. It is typically self-limiting over 7–14 days with vocal rest and hydration.

Unilateral or bilateral vocal fold paralysis — from recurrent laryngeal nerve injury after thyroid surgery, thoracic surgery, lung cancer, or viral neuritis — prevents proper cord adduction, resulting in a breathy or absent voice.
Large vocal fold polyps, nodules, papillomas, or malignancy can mechanically disrupt vibration, producing complete voice loss in severe cases; laryngeal cancer is the most serious underlying cause.
An intermittent task-specific dystonia of the laryngeal muscles in which phonation-related spasms interrupt voicing; it is neurological in origin and not the same as functional aphonia.

primarySymptoms: Complete absence of voiced sound — the patient can only whisper, mouth words, or produce a cough or laugh that has voice while conversational speech does not.
A strained or effortful attempt at speaking that produces no audible voice, sometimes with visible neck-muscle tension.
Throat clearing and compensatory coughing as the patient tries to stimulate phonation.
A paradoxical ability to laugh, cry, or cough with a normal voice while conversational phonation remains impossible, which is characteristic of functional aphonia.
Sore or raw throat sensation accompanying inflammatory aphonia, often with pain on swallowing.
A sensation of a lump, tightness, or obstruction in the throat (globus sensation) that many patients report before or during voice loss.
Fatigue or pain around the larynx after trying to force voice out, particularly when the aphonia is functional and the patient is straining against a non-organic block.
Breathiness on expiration during attempted speech, indicating air escaping through incompletely approximated vocal folds.
Mild dysphagia or altered sensation when swallowing in cases with significant laryngeal swelling or paralysis.
Anxiety, social withdrawal, and difficulty at work, reflecting the functional impact of being unable to communicate normally.
earlyWarningSigns: Progressive hoarseness over days before voice disappears entirely — a warning that the vocal folds are under increasing stress.
Voice that fatigues rapidly and 'cuts out' during long conversations before becoming fully absent.
Increasingly frequent throat clearing or the feeling that something is sitting across the throat.
Whispering replacing normal speech as the path of least resistance, which if sustained can reinforce the loss of normal phonation.
emergencySigns: Sudden total voice loss accompanied by shortness of breath or stridor (noisy breathing), which may signal laryngeal oedema, anaphylaxis, or acute airway compromise — call emergency services.
Voice loss following direct neck trauma, which raises concern for laryngeal fracture.
Rapid-onset aphonia with unilateral facial drooping, arm weakness, or other neurological signs pointing to stroke.
Difficulty breathing or swallowing solids and liquids developing alongside aphonia, suggesting a mass or severe swelling compromising the airway.
Aphonia persisting beyond 2–3 weeks without improvement, which requires laryngoscopy to exclude malignancy.

causes.description: The most common cause of sudden temporary aphonia: viral upper respiratory infection causes diffuse inflammation and oedema of the vocal folds, preventing normal vibration. The voice is absent or severely reduced for 7–14 days and returns spontaneously as the infection resolves.

In functional aphonia, a psychological stressor — acute or chronic — triggers a dissociative block of voluntary phonation while non-volitional voicing (coughing, laughing) remains intact. The laryngeal musculature is structurally and neurologically normal; the problem lies in the neural pathway from intent to action.

The recurrent laryngeal nerve can be damaged during thyroid or parathyroid surgery, oesophageal surgery, thoracic procedures, or compressed by a thoracic aortic aneurysm or lung tumour. Unilateral paralysis causes breathy hypophonia; bilateral paralysis can cause severe aphonia with stridor.

Benign lesions such as large polyps, Reinke's oedema, papillomas, or granulomas can disrupt vibration sufficiently to abolish voice in severe cases; laryngeal carcinoma must be excluded in any adult with persistent voice loss, particularly smokers.

Laryngopharyngeal reflux deposits acid and pepsin on the posterior larynx, causing oedema, erythema, and contact granulomas; in severe cases the resulting inflammation can render normal vocal fold vibration impossible.

A focal task-specific dystonia in which involuntary laryngeal muscle spasms interrupt phonation; in the adductor form, spasms clamp the folds shut during voiced speech, producing voice interruptions that can progress to near-aphonia.

Extended screaming, shouting, or unrelieved heavy vocal loading can produce haemorrhage into a vocal fold or acute mucosal disruption, causing abrupt voice loss that persists until the injury resolves over days to weeks.

riskFactors.description: Women are 4–10 times more likely than men to present with functional aphonia, a disparity seen consistently across voice clinic populations and thought to reflect both psychological and sociocultural factors.

riskFactors.factor: History of acute stress or psychological trauma
riskFactors.description: Functional aphonia frequently follows an identifiable acute stressor — bereavement, relationship breakdown, or public speaking anxiety — and may persist as a learned response if not treated early.

Teachers, singers, call-centre workers, and other vocal professionals are at higher risk of inflammatory and mass-related aphonia because sustained heavy use predisposes to mucosal injury, nodules, and haemorrhage.

Tobacco smoke is the primary risk factor for laryngeal cancer, the most dangerous cause of persistent aphonia; alcohol use potentiates the risk. Smoking also causes Reinke's oedema, a diffuse swelling of both vocal folds.
Chronic acid and pepsin exposure to the posterior larynx damages the vocal fold mucosa; management of reflux reduces recurrent voice loss in susceptible individuals.

Surgical procedures near the course of the recurrent laryngeal nerve carry a 1–5% risk of temporary and 0.3–1% risk of permanent vocal fold paralysis, depending on the procedure and surgeon experience.
Viral laryngitis is the leading acute precipitant of aphonia; frequent respiratory infections, poor hand hygiene, and immune compromise increase the frequency of these episodes.

Psychological comorbidities are present in a substantial proportion of patients with functional aphonia and may perpetuate the voice loss if untreated; treatment of the underlying mood disorder facilitates voice recovery.

affectedDemographics: Working-age women aged 20–50, who account for the majority of functional aphonia cases
Professional voice users including teachers, singers, actors, and call-centre staff
Post-surgical patients — particularly after thyroidectomy or thoracic operations near the recurrent laryngeal nerve
Long-term smokers with a history of heavy tobacco use
Adults under acute psychosocial stress, including bereavement, relationship crisis, or work-related anxiety
Older adults with neurological conditions such as Parkinson's disease or post-stroke changes affecting the larynx
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of aphonia begins with a detailed history: the onset (sudden versus gradual), any preceding event, the presence of cough or laugh with voice (pointing strongly to functional cause), associated pain, smoking history, recent surgery, and neurological symptoms. Physical examination includes palpation of the neck for thyroid masses or lymphadenopathy. The cornerstone investigation is flexible fibre-optic laryngoscopy, performed by an otolaryngologist or laryngologist, which allows direct visualisation of the vocal folds at rest, during phonation, and during coughing or laughing. In functional aphonia, folds move normally during non-phonatory tasks but fail to adduct for speech; in paralytic aphonia, one or both folds are immobile; in inflammatory cases, the folds appear red and oedematous. Videostroboscopy adds information about mucosal wave and vibration quality, helping to detect subtle lesions or mucosal stiffness not visible on standard laryngoscopy. Acoustic voice analysis — measuring fundamental frequency, jitter, shimmer, and harmonics-to-noise ratio — quantifies the disorder and tracks treatment response. When paralysis is found, imaging with CT from skull base to thoracic inlet is required to identify the cause along the course of the recurrent laryngeal nerve. Where a mass is identified, biopsy under general anaesthesia is performed. Electromyography of the laryngeal muscles can help determine whether a paralysis is neurogenic and gauge prognosis for recovery. Psychological assessment is part of the workup in suspected functional cases, and speech-language pathology assessment is central to planning voice therapy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualisation of vocal fold structure and movement to distinguish functional, paralytic, inflammatory, and mass-related causes of aphonia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin flexible endoscope is passed through a nostril or the mouth after topical anaesthetic spray; it takes about 5 minutes and is uncomfortable but not painful. The clinician asks you to breathe, cough, laugh, and attempt speech to compare different laryngeal tasks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Slow-motion assessment of vocal fold vibration using a stroboscopic light source to detect mucosal lesions, stiffness, or pathological vibration patterns invisible on standard laryngoscopy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed with the same laryngoscope as above but with a strobe light synchronized to your vocal frequency; the clinician records the examination for detailed review.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Objective measurement of pitch, quality, and stability of any residual voice to quantify severity and track treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You speak or sustain a vowel into a microphone connected to analysis software; the session takes 10–15 minutes and requires no physical preparation.

diagnosticTests.test: CT scan of neck and thorax
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies causes of recurrent laryngeal nerve injury such as thyroid or lung masses, thoracic aortic aneurysm, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy when vocal fold paralysis is confirmed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in a CT scanner for 10–15 minutes; iodinated contrast is usually injected to enhance vascular and soft tissue structures. Allergies and kidney function are checked beforehand.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures electrical activity in the laryngeal muscles to confirm neurogenic paralysis, differentiate it from mechanical fixation, and estimate prognosis for recovery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fine-needle electrodes are placed in the laryngeal muscles through the neck under local anaesthetic; the procedure takes 20–30 minutes and can be mildly uncomfortable.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies comorbid anxiety, depression, somatisation tendency, or acute stressors that underpin or perpetuate functional aphonia, guiding the psychological component of treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Structured interview and validated questionnaires administered by a psychologist or liaison psychiatrist; the assessment takes 45–90 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is matched precisely to the underlying cause. For acute viral laryngitis, the mainstay is vocal rest — whispering is actively discouraged because it places paradoxical strain on the larynx — along with generous humidified hydration, steam inhalation, and simple analgesia. Antibiotics are not indicated for viral disease but may be added if bacterial superinfection is suspected. Functional aphonia responds dramatically well to voice therapy delivered by a speech-language pathologist: techniques such as resonant voice therapy, laryngeal manipulation, and the vegetative facilitation technique (exploiting preserved cough or laugh to prime phonation) achieve full voice return in 60–90% of patients within a handful of sessions, often in the very first session. Psychological support — cognitive-behavioural therapy or counselling — is added when psychosocial factors are prominent, and addressing the underlying stressor is essential to prevent recurrence. Vocal fold paralysis is managed by monitoring for spontaneous recovery for 6–12 months after nerve injury, combined with immediate voice therapy to improve compensation; if recovery is incomplete, injection laryngoplasty (injection of material beside the paralysed fold to medialise it) or surgical medialisation thyroplasty restore an adequate glottic closure. Laryngeal masses require microlaryngoscopy and phonomicrosurgery for benign lesions; malignant disease is managed by an oncology team according to stage, with radiotherapy the preferred function-preserving modality for early glottic cancer. Reflux-related aphonia is treated with high-dose proton pump inhibitors and strict dietary and positional measures for a minimum of 8–12 weeks. Spasmodic dysphonia (laryngeal dystonia) is treated with regular laryngeal botulinum toxin injections, which restore near-normal voice for 3–6 months per treatment cycle in about 90% of patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Voice therapy (speech-language pathology)
medicalTreatments.description: The primary and most effective treatment for functional aphonia and a key adjunct for all other causes; techniques include resonant voice therapy, the vegetative facilitation method, laryngeal manual therapy, and direct voice exercises. A trained speech-language pathologist designs an individualised programme.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Full voice restoration in 60–90% of functional aphonia cases, often within 1–4 sessions; voice improvement in 70–80% of patients with organic dysphonia who receive adjunct therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs)
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose PPIs (e.g. omeprazole 40 mg twice daily or lansoprazole 30 mg twice daily) are prescribed for 8–16 weeks in laryngopharyngeal reflux-related voice loss; dietary modification and head-of-bed elevation are essential co-interventions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Objective laryngeal improvement in approximately 60–70% of patients with LPR-related voice disorders when PPIs are combined with dietary and lifestyle changes.

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin (Botox) injections
medicalTreatments.description: Injected into the thyroarytenoid muscles under electromyographic or laryngoscopic guidance for adductor spasmodic dysphonia (laryngeal dystonia); reduces involuntary spasms and restores functional phonation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Voice improvement in approximately 90% of adductor spasmodic dysphonia patients; effect lasts 3–6 months per injection cycle.

medicalTreatments.name: Vocal fold injection laryngoplasty
medicalTreatments.description: A non-surgical office or theatre procedure in which hyaluronic acid, fat, calcium hydroxylapatite, or carboxymethylcellulose is injected alongside a paralysed vocal fold to push it toward the midline and restore glottic closure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves voice and reduces breathiness in approximately 70–85% of suitable unilateral paralysis cases; temporary materials allow reassessment before committing to permanent surgical augmentation.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids (systemic or inhaled)
medicalTreatments.description: Short systemic steroid courses (e.g. dexamethasone 4–8 mg daily for 3–5 days) reduce severe laryngeal oedema rapidly and are used in severe acute laryngitis with airway compromise or as a bridge in professional voice users; inhaled steroids can worsen dysphonia and are generally avoided unless treating underlying asthma.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant reduction in laryngeal oedema within 24–48 hours in acute inflammatory cases; evidence base from randomised trials is limited but clinical effect is well established.

surgicalOptions.name: Medialisation thyroplasty
surgicalOptions.description: A permanent surgical procedure in which a silicone or Gore-Tex implant is placed through a window in the thyroid cartilage to permanently medialise a paralysed vocal fold; performed under local anaesthesia so the voice can be adjusted intraoperatively.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Voice improvement in approximately 80–90% of unilateral vocal fold paralysis patients; considered the gold standard for permanent medialisation.

surgicalOptions.name: Microlaryngoscopy and phonomicrosurgery
surgicalOptions.description: Performed under general anaesthesia using an operating microscope and microsurgical instruments to remove benign lesions (polyps, nodules, papillomas, granulomas, cysts) from the vocal folds while preserving the superficial lamina propria to maintain voice quality.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Good to excellent voice outcomes in 75–90% of carefully selected cases with benign lesions; recurrence rates depend on the underlying cause.

surgicalOptions.name: Laser laryngoscopy (CO2 laser)
surgicalOptions.description: CO2 laser delivered through a microlaryngoscope is used to vaporise recurrent respiratory papillomatosis, scar tissue, or certain benign lesions with minimal collateral thermal damage to surrounding vocal fold mucosa.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Controls papillomatosis recurrence and maintains airway in most patients; does not cure HPV-related disease but reduces frequency of regrowth procedures.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vocal hygiene programme
alternativeTreatments.description: A structured set of behaviours — adequate hydration (8 glasses of water daily), avoidance of throat clearing, elimination of irritants, adequate sleep, and humidity control — reduces mucosal drying and irritation and supports recovery from inflammatory and functional aphonia.

alternativeTreatments.name: Steam inhalation and humidification
alternativeTreatments.description: Inhaling steam or using a room humidifier keeps laryngeal mucosa moist and reduces the viscosity of secretions, easing discomfort during acute laryngitis and facilitating mucosal healing.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Targets the maladaptive thought patterns and behavioural responses that perpetuate functional aphonia; most effective when combined with concurrent voice therapy rather than as a standalone treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: An otolaryngologist (ENT specialist) or laryngologist should assess any aphonia that does not resolve within 2–3 weeks, any voice loss associated with pain, difficulty breathing or swallowing, a neck lump, coughing blood, or a history of smoking. Laryngoscopy is essential because without it the structural state of the vocal folds is unknown and malignancy cannot be excluded. A speech-language pathologist specialising in voice disorders is central to both diagnosis and treatment, particularly for functional aphonia.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an otolaryngologist or laryngologist with specific training or subspecialty interest in voice disorders, ideally with access to videostroboscopy, acoustic analysis, and a multidisciplinary voice clinic including a speech-language pathologist and psychologist. For functional aphonia, a speech-language pathologist with experience in functional voice disorders is often the most important clinician. Ask whether the practice runs a joint voice clinic and whether they have experience treating the suspected cause.
preventionStrategies: Stay well hydrated — drink 6–8 glasses of water daily; the vocal fold mucosa depends on systemic hydration to maintain optimal viscoelasticity.
Avoid prolonged whispering, which places as much mechanical strain on the larynx as shouting and can propagate functional voice loss.
Use microphone amplification whenever possible if your work involves sustained loud voice use, reducing the force of vocal effort.
Treat acid reflux with dietary changes, weight management, and medications before it damages laryngeal mucosa.
Stop smoking — tobacco is the single most important modifiable risk factor for laryngeal cancer and chronic laryngeal inflammation.
Seek early psychological support after significant life stressors, particularly if voice changes begin — early intervention prevents functional aphonia from becoming entrenched.
lifestyleModifications: Drink water throughout the day rather than in large infrequent amounts; coffee and alcohol are mildly dehydrating and should be offset with extra water.
Reduce dietary caffeine, alcohol, fatty foods, and spicy foods if reflux is contributing to your voice loss.
Sleep with the head of the bed elevated 15–20 cm if laryngopharyngeal reflux is suspected.
Avoid environments with dusty, dry, or chemically polluted air without protective measures; wear a mask when in heavily polluted areas.
Take planned voice rest breaks during demanding vocal performances or teaching sessions.
Limit throat clearing, which traumatises the mucosa; instead, swallow silently or sip water when you feel the urge to clear.

recommended: Abundant water throughout the day — at least 6–8 glasses — to maintain mucosal hydration
Non-caffeinated herbal teas, particularly those with honey and lemon (soothing for inflamed mucosa)
High-fibre foods that support healthy weight and reduce reflux pressure
Foods rich in antioxidants such as fruits and vegetables to support mucosal repair
avoid: Carbonated drinks, which increase gastric pressure and promote reflux
Spicy, fatty, and acidic foods in LPR-related aphonia, particularly in the 3 hours before lying down
Alcohol, which dehydrates mucosa and relaxes the lower oesophageal sphincter
Excessive caffeine, which dries mucosa and can increase reflux

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise is compatible with recovery from aphonia and generally beneficial for overall health and stress reduction. During active acute laryngitis, strenuous exercise is best avoided because it increases respiratory demand and may worsen laryngeal drying. Aerobic exercise, yoga, and mindfulness practice are particularly helpful when functional aphonia has a significant stress or anxiety component, as they reduce the background arousal that perpetuates the conversion process.
dailyManagement: Follow the voice therapy exercises prescribed by your speech-language pathologist at least twice daily during active treatment.
Carry a water bottle and sip regularly rather than waiting to feel thirsty.
Use a whiteboard, notepad, or text-to-speech app as a temporary communication aid while you rest the voice — do not compensate with whispering.
Monitor for throat clearing urges and replace the habit with a silent swallow.
Keep a voice diary noting triggers, duration, and quality of any voice that returns, which helps your clinician adjust treatment.
Seek same-day review if breathlessness or stridor develops alongside the voice loss.
prognosis: The prognosis for aphonia depends heavily on cause. Acute viral laryngitis resolves completely in 90% of cases within 10–14 days with voice rest and hydration. Functional aphonia has an excellent prognosis with targeted voice therapy: 60–90% of patients achieve full voice restoration, many in the first session, and recurrence is uncommon when the psychological trigger is addressed. Vocal fold paralysis carries a more variable outlook: spontaneous recovery of function occurs in 60–70% of unilateral idiopathic cases within 6–12 months; when it does not, surgical medialisation restores a functional voice in 80–90% of patients. Reflux-related aphonia usually improves substantially with aggressive reflux management over 8–16 weeks. Laryngeal cancer survival depends on stage: early glottic cancer (T1) treated with radiotherapy has 5-year local control rates of 85–95% and voice is generally preserved; advanced disease requires combined modality treatment with poorer voice outcomes. Benign masses removed by phonomicrosurgery generally have good voice outcomes.
recoveryTimeline: Acute viral aphonia typically improves within a week and resolves fully within 2–3 weeks of onset. Functional aphonia often recovers within 1–4 voice therapy sessions — sometimes in a single session — though full consolidation of normal voice patterns may take 6–12 weeks of continued practice. Paralytic aphonia from nerve injury: initial spontaneous recovery is monitored for up to 6–12 months; voice therapy begins immediately. Surgical correction of non-recovering paralysis requires a 4–8 week post-procedural recovery for the voice to settle. Post-phonomicrosurgery voice rest is typically required for 5–7 days, with gradual return over 4–6 weeks.
complications: Persistently altered voice quality even after the underlying cause is treated, particularly if fibrosis or scarring has developed from repeated mucosal trauma or surgery.
Psychological consequences including social isolation, career disruption, anxiety, and depression, which are common when aphonia is prolonged and can perpetuate functional cases.
Aspiration and swallowing difficulties if bilateral vocal fold paralysis is present, because the folds normally also protect the airway during swallowing.
Laryngeal cancer progression if malignancy underlying aphonia is not diagnosed and treated promptly.
Recurrence of functional aphonia, particularly if psychosocial stressors are not resolved and voice therapy gains are not reinforced.
Airway compromise in severe bilateral paralysis or acute severe laryngeal oedema, which can be life-threatening without prompt intervention.

supportResources.name: NIDCD — Voice, Speech, and Language
supportResources.description: Patient education resources on voice disorders, including functional dysphonia and vocal fold paralysis, from the National Institute on Deafness and Other Communication Disorders.

supportResources.name: British Voice Association — Patient Information
supportResources.description: Resources for patients with voice disorders, including information on finding voice-specialist clinicians in the UK.

supportResources.name: The Voice Foundation
supportResources.description: Professional and patient resources on voice science, voice disorders, and treatment options from the leading international voice health organisation.

sources.title: Bhattacharyya N. The prevalence of voice problems among adults in the United States. Laryngoscope, 2014
Baker J. Psychogenic voice disorders and traumatic stress experience. Journal of Voice, 2003
NICE — Dysphonia (hoarse voice): clinical knowledge summary
Sulica L, Blitzer A (eds). Voice: Medical and Surgical Management. Thieme, 2005 — Vocal Fold Paralysis chapter
American Academy of Otolaryngology–HNS Clinical Practice Guideline: Hoarseness (Dysphonia). Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery, 2018
Stager SV et al. Behavioral treatment of functional dysphonia. Journal of Voice, 2015

confusedWithConditions.name: Dysphonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dysphonia means a changed or impaired voice that is still present; aphonia means voice is completely absent. Both can share causes, but aphonia is the more severe end of the spectrum.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dysarthria and anarthria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dysarthria is a motor speech disorder affecting articulation, resonance, and prosody due to neurological damage; in aphonia the larynx is specifically affected while articulation may be intact.

confusedWithConditions.name: Laryngeal cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Laryngeal cancer is a specific structural cause of aphonia; the overlap is that both present with voice loss, but cancer requires endoscopy and biopsy to diagnose and has its own management pathway.

relatedConditions.name: Dysphonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Dysphonia (impaired voice) and aphonia (absent voice) share causes and often occur along a continuum, with dysphonia preceding or following aphonia.

relatedConditions.name: Vocal cord paralysis
relatedConditions.relevance: Paralysis of one or both vocal folds is a direct structural cause of aphonia and is managed by the same laryngology team.

relatedConditions.name: Laryngeal cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Persistent aphonia in a smoker must prompt exclusion of laryngeal malignancy; the two share the clinical presentation of progressive voice loss.

relatedConditions.name: Laryngopharyngeal reflux
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic acid exposure to the larynx is a frequent contributor to voice loss and must be treated as part of aphonia management.

faqs.question: What is the difference between aphonia and dysphonia?
faqs.answer: Aphonia means the voice is completely absent — only a whisper is possible. Dysphonia means the voice is present but abnormal (hoarse, rough, breathy, or strained). Both can have the same underlying causes, but aphonia is the more severe presentation.

faqs.question: Can aphonia be psychosomatic?
faqs.answer: Yes. Functional (psychogenic) aphonia accounts for 30–50% of voice clinic cases. The larynx is structurally normal, but a psychological trigger prevents the brain from initiating voluntary phonation. It responds well to voice therapy and is not the patient's deliberate choice — it is an involuntary neurological conversion.

faqs.question: How is functional aphonia treated?
faqs.answer: Functional aphonia responds well to voice therapy with a speech-language pathologist. Techniques such as the vegetative facilitation method use preserved cough or laugh to prime normal phonation. Full voice restoration is achieved in 60–90% of patients, often within a handful of sessions.

faqs.question: Will my voice come back after vocal fold paralysis?
faqs.answer: Spontaneous recovery occurs in 60–70% of unilateral idiopathic paralysis cases within 6–12 months. Voice therapy starts immediately to improve compensation. If recovery is incomplete, injection laryngoplasty or surgical medialisation restores a functional voice in 80–90% of patients.

faqs.question: How long does aphonia last with laryngitis?
faqs.answer: Aphonia caused by acute viral laryngitis typically improves within 7–10 days and resolves completely within 2–3 weeks with vocal rest, hydration, and steam inhalation. Voice should not be forced during recovery, and whispering should be avoided as it strains the inflamed folds.

faqs.question: Is losing your voice completely an emergency?
faqs.answer: Total voice loss alone is rarely an emergency, but seek immediate care if it is accompanied by difficulty breathing, stridor, throat tightness, or inability to swallow. These signs suggest airway compromise, anaphylaxis, or bilateral vocal fold paralysis — all of which need urgent evaluation.

faqs.question: Why can I cough or laugh with a voice but not speak?
faqs.answer: This is the hallmark of functional aphonia. Coughing and laughing are reflexive acts controlled by pathways that bypass the voluntary phonation circuit that is disrupted in functional aphonia. Finding a preserved cough voice strongly suggests the larynx is structurally intact and that treatment with voice therapy will work.

faqs.question: Can acid reflux cause you to lose your voice?
faqs.answer: Yes. Laryngopharyngeal reflux deposits acid and pepsin on the vocal fold mucosa, causing swelling, redness, and sometimes granulomas. In severe cases the resulting inflammation can abolish phonation. Treatment with high-dose proton pump inhibitors and dietary changes over 8–16 weeks usually restores the voice.

faqs.question: Can whispering help rest the voice during aphonia?
faqs.answer: No. Whispering is not true vocal rest — it places significant mechanical strain on the laryngeal muscles and mucosa, sometimes more than normal conversational speech. During recovery from acute laryngitis or functional aphonia, either speak in your normal voice or use written communication; do not whisper.

faqs.question: What tests will the doctor do for aphonia?
faqs.answer: The most important test is flexible laryngoscopy, which directly visualises the vocal folds and distinguishes functional, inflammatory, paralytic, and mass-related causes. Videostroboscopy assesses vibration quality. CT scan is added if paralysis is found to look for a cause along the nerve. Psychological assessment is part of the workup when functional aphonia is suspected.

faqs.question: Can a thyroid operation cause voice loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. The recurrent laryngeal nerve, which controls vocal fold movement, runs very close to the thyroid gland. Thyroidectomy carries a 1–5% risk of temporary and 0.3–1% risk of permanent vocal fold paralysis. Persistent voice changes after thyroid surgery should be assessed with laryngoscopy.

faqs.question: Can aphonia be a sign of cancer?
faqs.answer: Persistent aphonia, especially in a smoker, must prompt urgent laryngoscopy to exclude laryngeal cancer. Laryngeal cancer typically presents with progressive hoarseness that worsens over weeks to months. A voice change lasting more than 3 weeks without explanation needs endoscopic assessment.

faqs.question: What is the role of botulinum toxin in voice loss?
faqs.answer: Botulinum toxin (Botox) injected into the laryngeal muscles is the treatment of choice for adductor spasmodic dysphonia (laryngeal dystonia), a specific neurological cause of voice interruption that can progress to near-aphonia. It improves voice in about 90% of patients and the effect lasts 3–6 months per injection.

faqs.question: How do I find a voice specialist?
faqs.answer: Ask your GP for referral to an ENT (otolaryngologist) or laryngologist with a specific interest in voice disorders, ideally one who runs a multidisciplinary voice clinic alongside a speech-language pathologist. Voice Foundation (voicefoundation.org) and the British Voice Association maintain directories of trained clinicians.

faqs.question: What is medialisation thyroplasty?
faqs.answer: Medialisation thyroplasty is a surgical procedure in which an implant is placed beside a paralysed vocal fold to push it toward the midline, restoring glottic closure and voice. It is performed under local anaesthetic so the surgeon can adjust voice quality intraoperatively. About 80–90% of patients achieve significant voice improvement.

faqs.question: Can children get aphonia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children most commonly lose their voice due to acute viral laryngitis, which resolves spontaneously. Functional aphonia can also occur in school-aged children, often in association with school-related stress. A child with persistent voice loss, stridor, or a mass in the throat needs prompt ENT assessment to exclude papillomatosis or other structural causes.

faqs.question: Does voice therapy really work for functional aphonia?
faqs.answer: Evidence supports voice therapy strongly for functional aphonia: 60–90% of patients achieve full voice return, frequently in the first session when the speech-language pathologist uses facilitation techniques. The key is identifying preserved reflexive voicing (cough, laugh) and using that to trigger normal phonation.

faqs.question: How do I prevent recurrence of functional aphonia?
faqs.answer: Recurrence is reduced by completing the full voice therapy programme, addressing underlying psychological triggers with counselling or CBT, maintaining good vocal hygiene habits, and managing reflux or other physical contributors. Early return to voice therapy at the first sign of recurrence prevents re-entrenchment.

faqs.question: What lifestyle changes help the voice recover?
faqs.answer: Stay well hydrated with water rather than caffeine or alcohol, avoid whispering, eliminate cigarette smoke exposure, manage acid reflux with diet and elevation, use amplification to reduce vocal effort at work, and manage stress which may perpetuate functional voice loss. Humidifying your bedroom air also reduces mucosal drying overnight.

metaDescription: Aphonia is complete voice loss from laryngitis, paralysis, or psychogenic causes. Learn symptoms, diagnosis, and treatments to restore your voice.
